channel.title: Testing on cekrem.github.io
channel.description: Recent content in Testing on cekrem.github.io

item.title: An Elm Primer: Testing Strategies
item.description:
This chapter might be the most freeing one in the book. If you’ve spent real time writing React tests, you know the ritual: mocks, cleanup, async wrappers, act() warnings. In Elm, testing is just calling pure functions and checking the output.

Believe it or not, the book is actually closing in on being finished. We’re in Part IV now (Production Readiness), and the finish line is genuinely in sight. If you want to follow along for the final stretch, remember to subscribe.

item.title: Dependency Inversion in React: Building Truly Testable Components
item.description:
In the world of React development, we often find ourselves writing components that are tightly coupled to their dependencies. This makes testing difficult, maintenance a challenge, and change nearly impossible. The Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) offers a way out of this mess, but how do we apply it effectively in React?

Note: For a more backend-oriented take on Dependency Inversion, check out my previous post on Dependency Inversion in Go Using Plugins.

item.title: Double Loop TDD: Building My Blog Engine the Right Way (part 2)
item.description:
From Hugo to Kotlin: The Journey Continues Link to heading

In my previous post, embarked on a bold and ambitious journey to replace Hugo with a custom Kotlin-based blog engine built using Clean Architecture principles (to the letter!). Today, I want to dive deeper into the development process, specifically how I’m using Double Loop TDD to ensure the quality and maintainability of the system. Again, I’m trying to go all-in, basically to see how far is too far, and to learn and explore.
